original_abstract:
The distribution of ions at the air/water interface is fundamental to a wide array of natural phenomena. Conventional wisdom, supported by several studies, suggests that large, polarizable ions are "surface-active"—meaning they reside at the very top of the water surface. This arrangement is thought to induce electric fields that organize the structure of the interfacial water. In this study, we challenge this established model by combining surface-specific heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (HD-VSFG) with neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. 

Our findings reveal that for typical electrolyte solutions, ions actually reside in a subsurface region rather than at the immediate surface. This creates a stratified interface consisting of two distinct layers: an outermost layer that is depleted of ions and a subsurface layer that is ion-enriched. We demonstrate that this surface stratification, rather than a traditional electric double layer, is the primary driver of water reorganization at the air/water interface. This new microscopic picture provides a unifying framework for understanding ion behavior at one of nature's most ubiquitous boundaries.
humanized_abstract:
Ion arrangement at the air/water interface is important for many physical phenomena in nature. Typically, large polarizable ions are considered to be “surface active”, i.e., they sit at the very surface of water. This preferential site promotes the arrangement of interfacial water by generating an electric field. However, in this study, we use surface-specific heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (HD-VSFG) and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations to study the relationship between the ion configuration and interfacial structure.

Ions in Solution Do Not Reside at the Air/Water Interface Our results show that for most electrolyte solutions studied, ions do not reside at the air/water surface. Instead, the interface is composed of an ion-poor surface layer and an ion-rich subsurface layer. Importantly, our results show that the surface stratification we observe, rather than the electric double layer (EDL), is responsible for the water structuring observed at the air/water interface. Our findings provide a new understanding of ion behavior at one of the most important biological interfaces.

However, in this study, we use surface-specific heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (HD-VSFG) and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations to study the relationship between the ion configuration and interfacial structure.

Ions in Solution Do Not Reside at the Air/Water Interface Our results show that for most electrolyte solutions studied, ions do not reside at the air/water surface. Instead, the interface is composed of an ion-poor surface layer and an ion-rich subsurface layer. Importantly, our results show that the surface stratification we observe, rather than the electric double layer (EDL), is responsible for the water structuring observed at the air/water interface. Our findings provide a new understanding of ion behavior at one of the most important biological interfaces.
